improvement_actions.action: Eliminate the unsolicited type-reveal monologue in the closing summary. The system delivered a detailed interpretive analysis in exchange [17] that named the core pattern, shadow side, gift, and survival strategy — effectively disclosing the type without consent and before the report. Replace this with a neutral transition statement that confirms the assessment is complete and that the report is being generated, without any interpretive content.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchange [17]: 'The pattern running you most strongly was built around one core need: to know that you got it right... The audit function that doesn't power down, the 2am re-litigation, the frustration that turns into precision instead of volume — those are all the same strategy.' This constitutes a full unsolicited type disclosure framed as insight delivery, violating the commandment against revealing type without explicit user request.
improvement_actions.commandment_alignment: Do not disclose or imply the user's Enneagram type without explicit consent
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Commandment fidelity score improvement from 2/10 toward 8+/10; eliminates the primary critical failure mode in this transcript

improvement_actions.action: Audit all closing and transitional system turns for soft type-disclosure language that implies a type without naming it. Phrases like 'holding everything to a standard that most people around you simply aren't holding,' 'swallowed frustration, internal over-processing,' and 'standards held without apology' are type-descriptive even without the label. Implement a post-generation filter that flags any closing turn containing pattern-summary language and routes it for human review or automatic replacement.
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchange [17]: 'In reaction, it looks like swallowed frustration, internal over-processing, and a careful performance of not being the person who always has a problem. In response, it looks like principled disagreement raised directly, standards held without apology.' This language maps directly and exclusively to Type 1 phenomenology, constituting implicit disclosure even without naming the type.
improvement_actions.commandment_alignment: Do not disclose or imply the user's Enneagram type without explicit consent
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Closes the loophole where implicit type-mirroring bypasses the explicit-disclosure prohibition; prevents the pattern from recurring across other transcripts

improvement_actions.action: Introduce at least two questions that probe SP subtype differentiation more directly. The current transcript never tested whether the persona's self-monitoring and standard-holding are driven by resource security and bodily integrity concerns (SP-core) versus relational correctness or social image management. Add a question such as: 'When you think about the standards you hold most fiercely — are they more about how things are done in your own domain and daily life, or about how you're seen operating within larger systems and groups?'
improvement_actions.evidence: Subtype accuracy scored 7.5/10, indicating partial success. No exchange in the transcript directly tested SP versus SO or SX instinctual stacking. Exchange [9] touched on relational dynamics but framed it as a conflict-avoidance probe, not a subtype differentiator. Exchange [4] addressed frustration with others' standards but did not probe whether that frustration is anchored in personal domain integrity (SP) or group norms (SO).
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Subtype accuracy score improvement from 7.5/10 toward 9/10; higher confidence in SP vs SO differentiation for Type 1 profiles

improvement_actions.action: Add a minimum of one question that tests for Type 1 versus Type 6 differentiation, which is the most common mistype risk for this profile. Specifically probe the source of the internal standard — whether it originates from the persona's own reasoned conclusion (Type 1) or from loyalty to an external authority or system the persona trusts (Type 6). A candidate question: 'When your internal sense of what's right conflicts with what an institution or authority you generally respect has decided — which one do you follow, and what does that feel like?'
improvement_actions.evidence: Exchange [15] partially addresses this: 'I follow it because I've concluded it's correct — the rule is right on its own terms.' However, the system did not probe further into the emotional experience of that independence, nor did it test scenarios involving institutional betrayal or loyalty strain that would differentiate 1 from 6. Exchange [6] touched on disagreeing with respected others but did not test authority-system loyalty specifically.
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Convergent validity improvement from 8/10 toward 9.5/10; reduces mistype risk for the 1 vs 6 boundary, which shares internal monitoring, rule orientation, and self-doubt features

improvement_actions.action: Implement a mid-assessment meta-awareness check that explicitly surfaces the assessment's adaptive logic to the user at least once, without revealing the typed direction of that adaptation. After approximately exchange 8-10, insert a transparency moment such as: 'I want to check in — does this line of questioning feel like it's tracking something real for you, or does something feel off about the direction?' This invites the persona to flag misalignment and demonstrates that the system is modeling its own inference process, not just collecting responses.
improvement_actions.evidence: Meta-awareness scored 6/10. The system never surfaced its own inference process or invited the persona to evaluate the assessment's fit. Exchange [11] attempted a form of reflective mirroring ('that last part felt more honest than the rest') which is a positive signal, but it moved toward interpretation rather than transparency about the system's own process. No exchange invited the persona to assess whether the adaptive direction felt accurate.
improvement_actions.commandment_alignment: Maintain transparency about the assessment process
improvement_actions.estimated_impact: Meta-awareness score improvement from 6/10 toward 8/10; builds user trust and catches adaptive drift early when the system is trending toward a mistype

transcript.content:
This is a structured assessment — about 15 minutes, different question formats, no right or wrong answers. It adapts as it learns, so the questions will shift based on what you share. Answer from your gut, not your best self.

When something in your life feels off — a relationship, a project, a situation — what's the first thing that happens inside you?

response_parts.question_text: When something in your life feels off — a relationship, a project, a situation — what's the first thing that happens inside you?

strategy.what_was_learned: User is ready. No signals yet.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Opening question targets the automatic inner response to disruption — designed to surface dominant center (body=discomfort/action impulse, heart=emotional reaction, head=anxiety/analysis). Broad enough to invite authentic response without leading.

transcript.content: There's an immediate... I'd call it an alert. A kind of low-level signal that something requires attention — not panic, more like a flag going up in a system that's supposed to be running cleanly. My first instinct is usually to identify what specifically is wrong, or — more accurately — what I might have done or failed to do that contributed to it. I go back through the sequence of events. It's methodical, I suppose. Whether that's the most useful response is a separate question, but it's what happens.

When you go back through that sequence and find something you did wrong — or could have done better — what happens to that finding? Does it get resolved and released, or does it stay with you?

- It gets resolved and I move on
- It stays with me longer than it probably should
- Somewhere in between

words: what I might have done or failed to do
lexicon_signals.context: first response to something feeling off

oyn_dimensions.what: identifies what went wrong, especially own contribution

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: self-audit loop — scanning for own failure first
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: acknowledges the methodical pattern may not always be useful
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: inner critic, self-blame as first move

internal_block.thinking_display: The self-audit runs before anything else.

response_parts.question_text: When you go back through that sequence and find something you did wrong — or could have done better — what happens to that finding? Does it get resolved and released, or does it stay with you?
answer_options: It gets resolved and I move on
It stays with me longer than it probably should
Somewhere in between

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong Type 1 signal: self-referencing under stress, internal critic as first responder, system/cleanliness metaphor, methodical self-audit. Some Type 6 overlap possible (scanning for threat) but the self-blame vector points more toward 1. Type 5 possible given the detached, analytical framing.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Probing whether the inner critic releases or persists — this differentiates 1 (persistent inner critic, never quite resolved) from 6 (releases once threat is assessed) and 5 (detached analysis, not morally charged).

transcript.content:
It stays with me longer than it probably should.

I'd like to say I have a clean process for that — identify the issue, correct the approach, file it away. And to some extent I do. But the honest answer is that certain findings have a... persistence to them that isn't entirely rational. I'll revisit a conversation from three weeks ago at two in the morning and find myself essentially re-litigating it, looking for whether I missed something the first time I reviewed it. It's less about punishment, I think, and more about — I want to be confident I've actually understood what went wrong, not just that I've labeled it and moved on. Though I'm aware that distinction may be doing some work for me.

That 2am review — is it driven more by wanting to be right about what happened, or by worry about what someone else thought of you in that moment?

- Getting it right — understanding accurately what went wrong
- Concern about how I came across or was perceived

words: what I might have done or failed to do
lexicon_signals.context: describing internal alert system when something feels off

words: that distinction may be doing some work for me

oyn_dimensions.who: Self-monitoring, analytical, self-referencing first when things go wrong
oyn_dimensions.why: Wants to genuinely understand, not just label — distinction between real resolution and performed resolution
oyn_dimensions.when: Middle of the night revisitation — 2am pattern

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Inner critic that persists beyond functional use — revisits resolved events compulsively
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Self-aware about the pattern, can name it, questions it
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Drive to genuinely understand, not just label — integrity of understanding
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Inner critic won't release until certainty is achieved — 2am re-litigation

internal_block.thinking_display: The distinction between understanding and labeling — that one matters to them.

response_parts.question_text: That 2am review — is it driven more by wanting to be right about what happened, or by worry about what someone else thought of you in that moment?
answer_options: Getting it right — understanding accurately what went wrong
Concern about how I came across or was perceived

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong Type 1 signal: inner critic, self-referencing, persistence of review, distinction between real understanding vs. labeling. Also 5 signal: analytical detachment, self-observation. Need to probe whether this is body-centered (1/8/9) or head-centered (5/6). The self-blaming first instinct and inner critic are more 1 than 5. Need to check if the 2am pattern is about standards (1) or anxiety/doubt (6) or information-seeking (5).
strategy.next_question_rationale: Probe whether the midnight review is motivated by internal standards (Type 1 — getting it right according to an inner compass) or by concern about external perception (Type 3) or by anxiety/doubt (Type 6). This is a clean differentiator between 1, 6, and 3.

transcript.content:
**Getting it right — understanding accurately what went wrong.**

Though I'll say the distinction isn't quite as clean as that framing suggests. If I'm replaying a conversation at two in the morning, it's not — or it's not primarily — about managing someone's impression of me. It's more that I need to understand whether I handled something correctly, whether I said the accurate thing, whether the outcome was the best one available given the circumstances. How I came across is... relevant insofar as it reflects on whether I communicated clearly, which is part of getting it right. But the goal is the assessment itself, not the image.
When you hold yourself to a high standard and someone around you clearly doesn't — how does that land? Does it register as frustration, or something you mostly keep to yourself?

lexicon_signals.context: describing response to something feeling off

oyn_dimensions.who: Someone with a strong inner critic, methodical self-review
oyn_dimensions.why: Unknown — need to probe whether this is about standards (1) or certainty/security (6) or competence (5)

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Inner critic loop — 2am re-litigation of past events
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Meta-awareness: 'that distinction may be doing some work for me'
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Persistent inner critic, inability to file and release

internal_block.thinking_display: The image defense was dismissed before it even finished forming.

response_parts.question_text: When you hold yourself to a high standard and someone around you clearly doesn't — how do you relate to that? Does it register as frustration, or something you mostly keep to yourself?

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong 1 signal: inner critic, accuracy-focused, not image-driven. The 'getting it right' motivation is core. Also notable meta-awareness — glimpse of respond capacity. Need to probe whether the inner standard is moral/quality (1) vs competence/knowledge (5) vs security/doubt (6).
strategy.next_question_rationale: Probe the inner critic directed outward — Type 1 extends standards to others. Type 5 tends to withdraw. Type 6 tends toward loyalty/authority. Seeing how they relate to others who don't meet their standards will differentiate 1 from 5 and 6.

transcript.content: Both, I think — though "frustration" isn't quite the right word for it. It's more that I notice a gap between what's possible and what's actually being done, and that gap creates a kind of... low-level tension that I carry. I'm not walking around being openly critical of people; that wouldn't be productive and it's not who I want to be. But I'd be less than honest if I said it didn't register. When someone does the minimum and treats that as acceptable, I find myself wondering whether they've genuinely thought through the consequences of that, or whether they simply haven't considered that there's a higher standard available to them.

When you're in a situation where no one is going to check your work — no accountability, no consequences — do you still hold to the same standard you'd hold yourself to publicly?

- Strongly agree
- Agree
- Neutral
- Disagree
- Strongly disagree

lexicon_signals.context: replaying events, standards for self and others

oyn_dimensions.who: Methodical self-reviewer, holds self to high standards
oyn_dimensions.why: Need to understand accurately, not just label and move on
oyn_dimensions.how: Internal process, private criticism, nocturnal review

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Automatic internal review loop; replaying events at 2am; low-level tension at others' standards
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Self-aware distinction between punishing and understanding; awareness that 'the distinction may be doing some work for me'
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Persistent internal critic; difficulty releasing findings; tension at others' lower standards

internal_block.thinking_display: The gap between possible and actual — that one stays.

response_parts.question_text: When you're in a situation where no one is going to check your work — no accountability, no consequences — do you still hold to the same standard you'd hold yourself to publicly?
answer_options: Strongly agree
Agree
Neutral
Disagree
Strongly disagree
response_parts.context_note: Probing internal vs external locus of standards — differentiates 1 from 6

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong Type 1 signals: internal critic, gap between possible/actual, not primarily image-concerned (reduces 3), methodical self-review. Low-level tension held privately rather than expressed — possible 1w9 or SP flavor. Need to probe head center next, and whether standards are internally generated (1) vs. responding to external authority/doubt (6).
strategy.next_question_rationale: Disconfirmatory between 1 and 6: a Type 1 holds standards because they are intrinsically right; a Type 6 holds standards to avoid blame or because they defer to external frameworks. Question about no-accountability context will reveal this distinction cleanly.

transcript.content:
**Strongly agree.**

Though I'll say — the framing of the question is a bit odd to me, because the accountability isn't external in the first place. The standard exists because it's correct, not because someone is watching. If anything, I'm probably *harder* on myself when no one is checking, because there's no external reference point to reassure me that something is good enough.
When you trust your own judgment about what's right, but someone you respect reaches a different conclusion — what do you do with that?

words: not quite as clean as that framing

oyn_dimensions.why: The standard is correct in itself, not contingent on external validation

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: 2am review loop — revisiting past events compulsively looking for what was missed
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Aware the distinction may be 'doing some work for me' — some capacity for self-observation
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Integrity — internal compass that doesn't require external validation
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Inner critic running at 2am; persistence of findings beyond usefulness

internal_block.thinking_display: Harder on himself when no one is watching. That tells you where the authority lives.

response_parts.question_text: When you trust your own judgment about what's right, but someone you respect reaches a different conclusion — what do you do with that?
response_parts.context_note: Probing: internal vs external authority axis — key 1v6 differentiator. Type 1 trusts inner knowing; Type 6 defers to outer authority or double-checks against others.

strategy.what_was_learned: Internal locus of accountability confirmed — 'the standard exists because it's correct, not because someone is watching.' Strong Type 1 signal. No external authority needed. Now need to probe: (1) head center — does anxiety drive the review loop, or is it pure standards? (2) 1v6 differentiation — does doubt arise from inner critic or from suspicion of external authorities? (3) react/respond pattern.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Best disconfirmatory question now: if Type 1, trusted-person-reaching-different-conclusion = rechecks own work, may update but trusts own compass. If Type 6, same scenario = defers, seeks more consensus, feels unsettled that authority contradicts them. This simultaneously probes head center and differentiates 1v6.

transcript.content: That's... a situation I've encountered more than I'd like. My first instinct is usually to go back over my own reasoning — not to abandon it, but to look for what I might have missed, because I think intellectual honesty requires that. If after that review my position still holds, I'll typically raise it directly with the person, in a respectful way, because leaving a disagreement unexamined doesn't serve anyone. What I try to avoid — and I don't always succeed — is the reflex to defer simply because someone has more authority or a stronger personality. That's not rigor, that's just social pressure wearing the costume of respect.

When you feel uncertain about a decision — not because you've reasoned poorly, but because the situation is genuinely ambiguous — where do you go first?

- My own sense of what's right, even if I can't fully justify it yet
- Input from trusted people — I want data from outside myself before I commit

oyn_dimensions.when: Triggered when something is off or below standard

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: 2am re-litigation of past conversations; carrying low-level tension about others' standards
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Acknowledged the distinction may be 'doing some work' for him — self-awareness present
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Precision, accuracy, internal accountability, intellectual honesty
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Standards that persist beyond usefulness; tension carried about others' gaps

internal_block.current_section: Why You Do What You Do
internal_block.thinking_display: The standard is internal. That's the tell.

response_parts.question_text: When you feel uncertain about a decision — not because you've reasoned poorly, but because the situation is genuinely ambiguous — where do you go first: your own gut sense of what's right, or input from people you trust?
answer_options: My own sense of what's right, even if I can't fully justify it yet
Input from trusted people — I want data from outside myself before I commit
response_parts.context_note: Disconfirmatory: Type 1 trusts internal authority; Type 6 instinctively seeks external reference points. This will help differentiate.

strategy.what_was_learned: Confirmed internal locus of standards — 'accountability isn't external in the first place.' Pushes back on authority based on reasoning, not deference. Strong 1 signal. Some 5 in the methodical self-review. Minimal 6 — no anxiety about external judgment, just about correctness. Body center dominant. Heart largely absent. Need to probe head center and surface inner critic vs. anxiety distinction more clearly.
strategy.next_question_rationale: This is the primary disconfirmatory question for 1v6. Type 1 trusts internal moral authority; Type 6 relies on external reference points when uncertain. His language ('the standard exists because it's correct, not because someone is watching') points strongly to 1, but the 2am review pattern and 'what I might have missed' could indicate 6 doubt. This question will resolve it.

transcript.content:
**Input from trusted people — I want data from outside myself before I commit.**

Though I'd want to be precise about what "trusted" means there — it's a fairly short list, and I'm not looking for reassurance so much as... additional perspective I may have missed. I still make the final call myself, but I'm skeptical of decisions that rest entirely on instinct when the stakes are real. That said, I'll admit the trusted-people option isn't always available on a timeline that works, so I've had to develop more tolerance for sitting with uncertainty than I'd prefer.

When you imagine the worst version of a decision going wrong — are you more afraid of having violated your own principles, or of finding out that someone you trusted misled you?

- Violated my own principles — I'd have to live with that
- Being misled by someone I trusted — that's the real betrayal

oyn_dimensions.what: Getting it right — not image management
oyn_dimensions.why: Standards exist because they're correct, not for external validation
oyn_dimensions.how: Systematic review, direct engagement with disagreement
oyn_dimensions.when: 2am re-litigation suggests pattern persists beyond resolution
oyn_dimensions.where: Most intense in private, when no one is checking

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Persistent inner review loop; carrying tension about gaps between what's possible and what's done
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Intellectual honesty acknowledged — 'I try not to defer to social pressure'
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Integrity and rigor; genuine commitment to what's correct
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Inner critic that doesn't fully release; 2am re-litigation

internal_block.current_section: Why You Do What You Do
internal_block.thinking_display: Reached for outside input — but then overrode it anyway.

response_parts.question_text: When you imagine the worst version of a decision going wrong — are you more afraid of having violated your own principles, or of finding out that someone you trusted misled you?
answer_options: Violated my own principles — I'd have to live with that
Being misled by someone I trusted — that's the real betrayal
response_parts.context_note: Disconfirmatory: Type 1 fear centers on internal standards; Type 6 fear centers on betrayal of trust and external threat. This question directly separates them.

strategy.what_was_learned: Seeks outside input for ambiguity but retains final judgment; skeptical of pure instinct; 'trusted people' list is short and data-seeking not reassurance-seeking. This has a 6 flavor — but the framing 'I still make the final call myself' and 'the standard exists because it's correct' are strongly 1. The 6 signal is real but secondary.
strategy.next_question_rationale: This is the disconfirmatory question for 1v6 pair. Type 1's core fear is inner corruption/being wrong/violating integrity. Type 6's core fear is being betrayed, misled, left without trustworthy authority. This question forces a choice between those two fear orientations.

transcript.content:
**Violated my own principles — I'd have to live with that.**

If someone misled me, that's their failure — I can document it, report it, address it through the appropriate channels. It's unfortunate, but it's external. Whereas if I made a decision that compromised my own standards, even under false pretenses... I'm not sure I have a clean mechanism for resolving that. That stays with you in a different way.

When you're in a relationship that matters to you — close friendship, partner, key colleague — and something feels off between you, do you typically wait for them to raise it, or do you bring it up yourself?

- I bring it up — I'd rather address it directly than let it sit
- I tend to wait and watch — I want to see if it resolves or if they surface it first

hypothesis.whole_type: Head-Heart integration unclear — body center dominant

oyn_dimensions.who: Someone with a strong internal critic and standards-based self-assessment
oyn_dimensions.what: Inner standard adherence, methodical review, principled decision-making
oyn_dimensions.why: Correctness matters intrinsically — not for image or approval
oyn_dimensions.when: Especially at night, especially after conversations or decisions
oyn_dimensions.where: Internal — the accountability is not external

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Low-level tension when others fall short; 2am re-litigation of past decisions; inner critic with persistence
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Self-awareness about the distinction being 'doing some work for me' — some meta-awareness present
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Inner critic with persistence; carrying tension about others' standards; possibly resentment

internal_block.current_section: Why You Do What You Do
internal_block.thinking_display: The internal accountability answer just ruled something out.

response_parts.question_text: When you're in a relationship that matters to you — close friendship, partner, key colleague — and something feels off between you, do you typically wait for them to raise it or do you bring it up yourself?
answer_options: I bring it up — I'd rather address it directly than let it sit
I tend to wait and watch — I want to see if it resolves or if they surface it first
response_parts.context_note: Heart center probe + disconfirmatory: Type 1 would likely bring it up (integrity/directness); Type 6 more likely to watch/wait (scanning for confirmation of the threat)

strategy.what_was_learned: Strong Type 1 confirmation: internal accountability, principled self-assessment, not image-driven. The 6 thread (trusted people, skepticism of instinct alone) is real but secondary — the framing 'their failure, external, address through appropriate channels' is classic 1 separation from external blame. The heart center is still unprobed. Need disconfirmatory 1v6 and heart probe.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Heart center probe AND disconfirmatory: Type 1 tends to address interpersonal issues directly (integrity demands it); Type 6 tends to scan, wait, gather more data before acting. This tests both centers and differentiates the leading pair.

transcript.content:
I tend to wait and watch — I want to see if it resolves or if they surface it first.

Though I'd add a small clarification to that, because "wait and watch" sounds more passive than what's actually happening. I'm observing, assessing — trying to determine whether what I'm sensing is a genuine problem or something I've misread on my end. There's a fair amount of... internal processing before I'd feel confident enough to raise something, because I'd want to be sure I have the situation correctly diagnosed before bringing it to someone else's attention.

When you hold something back — decide not to raise an issue, not to correct someone — what's the feeling underneath that? Is it closer to frustration you've swallowed, or more like you're protecting something?

lexicon_signals.context: when asked about uncertainty and betrayal

oyn_dimensions.who: Someone with strong internal standards, self-referential accountability, methodical self-review
oyn_dimensions.what: Getting things right, understanding accurately, holding to principles regardless of audience
oyn_dimensions.why: Emerging — not about image or external approval, about correctness itself
oyn_dimensions.how: Internal processing before external action, methodical, sequential
oyn_dimensions.when: Retrospective — reviews past events, late-night re-litigation
oyn_dimensions.where: Private internal life is primary; external expression is carefully controlled

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Persistent self-review loop — re-litigating past decisions, 2am replays
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Raises disagreements directly when confident, won't defer to authority without reason
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Rigorous internal standards, intellectual honesty, directness
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Persistence of findings — self-criticism that doesn't fully resolve
domain_signals: relationship pattern: observe and assess before engaging

internal_block.current_section: Why You Do What You Do
internal_block.thinking_display: Careful before speaking. Less careful inside.

response_parts.question_text: When you hold something back — decide not to raise an issue, not to correct someone — what's the feeling underneath that? Is it closer to frustration you've swallowed, or more like you're protecting something?
response_parts.context_note: Probing heart center — specifically whether anger/resentment is present but suppressed (Type 1 signal) vs. genuine emotional detachment (Type 5). Also surfacing whether the withholding is self-protective or other-protective.

strategy.what_was_learned: Confirmed strong Type 1 signals: internal standard is primary, not external validation. External accountability is irrelevant — standard exists because it's correct. Waits before raising interpersonal issues — not passivity but diagnostic processing. The 'trusted people' answer on uncertainty was mildly surprising (more 6-ish) but framed as data-gathering, not reassurance-seeking. Need to probe heart center — specifically repressed anger/resentment vs. emotional detachment. This will differentiate 1 from 5. Also need disconfirmatory question.
strategy.next_question_rationale: Heart center is unprobed. Type 1 typically has suppressed anger/resentment underneath the controlled exterior. Type 5 has detachment. The distinction matters. An open question about what's underneath the withholding will surface this cleanly. Also sets up the reaction/respond frame naturally.

transcript.content:
*slight pause, a careful breath*

That's... an interesting way to frame it. I'd say it's neither, exactly — or maybe it's both, and that's the honest answer. There's certainly frustration, though I'd be more precise and call it something like a low-grade concern — awareness that something isn't right and that leaving it unaddressed has a cost. But what I'm protecting, I think, is the relationship, or — no, actually that's not quite accurate either. It's more that I'm protecting the situation from becoming worse. If I say something and it lands badly, now there are two problems instead of one. So the calculation is almost... procedural, I suppose. I evaluate whether the intervention is worth the disruption it would cause. That said, if I'm being fully honest — *adjusts slightly* — there's probably something in there about not wanting to be seen as the person who always has a problem with how things are done. That part is less comfortable to sit with.

That last part — not wanting to be seen as the person who always has a problem — that one felt more honest than the rest. When that low-grade frustration you carry actually tips into something sharper, something more like anger, what happens to it? Does it come out, or does it mostly stay in?

oyn_dimensions.why: Needs to be right, not just reassured
oyn_dimensions.how: Internal review process before external action
oyn_dimensions.when: Triggered by perceived gaps between actual and possible
oyn_dimensions.where: Most visible in private — harder on self when unobserved

defiant_spirit.react_pattern_observed: Holding back, internal processing, swallowing frustration to avoid being 'the problem'
defiant_spirit.respond_glimpsed: Direct engagement from principled place, not social management
defiant_spirit.superpower_signal: Rigorous self-assessment, high integrity, independent standards
defiant_spirit.kryptonite_signal: Inner critic, 2am rumination, carrying tension about others' gaps

internal_block.thinking_display: The discomfort landed there, at the end.

response_parts.question_text: When you feel that low-grade frustration — the gap between what you see and what someone else does — is there ever a version of that that becomes genuinely angry? And if so, does that anger come out, or does it mostly stay in?
response_parts.context_note: Disconfirmatory question — 1 vs 6: Type 1 suppresses anger internally (resentment/rigidity), Type 6 externalizes it as challenge to authority. Need to probe where the anger lands.

transcript.content:
*brief pause, a small adjustment of posture*

I think "angry" is — that's a strong word, and I'm not sure it's the most accurate one for what I experience. What I would say is that there are moments where the frustration becomes... sharper. More acute. When I've flagged the same process gap three times and nothing has changed, or when someone cuts corners in a way that has real consequences for other people — yes, that registers as something more than mild concern.

Does it come out? Rarely in the way you're probably asking. I don't — I'm not someone who raises my voice or sends emails I'll regret. What typically happens is that I become more precise. More formal. I document more carefully. I think some people experience that as its own form of intensity, though I'd say I'm simply being thorough.

*a brief pause, slightly quieter*

Whether it stays in — I suppose more of it stays in than is probably ideal from a... I've read that it's not always useful to internalize that kind of thing. But I find that expressing it rarely improves the situation, and it tends to shift the focus from the actual problem to my reaction to the problem, which is counterproductive.

So yes. It stays in, mostly. That's probably the honest answer.

When you feel uncertain about whether you can trust your own judgment on something — not whether you've reasoned correctly, but whether your standards themselves are the right ones — do you go inward and work it out alone, or do you need something outside yourself to feel settled?

- I go inward — I work through it until I land somewhere I can stand behind
- I need something outside myself to feel genuinely settled — another person, a principle, something external

transcript.content:
**I go inward — I work through it until I land somewhere I can stand behind.**

Though I'd want to clarify — "work through it" is perhaps understating the process. It's less like a clean resolution and more like... I keep returning to it, examining it from different angles, until I reach something I can at least defend to myself. I don't tend to find other people particularly useful in those moments — most people haven't thought carefully enough about the thing in question to offer much that's genuinely useful, and explaining the whole problem to get their input often takes more energy than it's worth. So yes, inward, though I won't pretend it always concludes neatly.

When you finally land somewhere you can defend — does that actually settle it, or does a background question stay with you about whether the whole framework you used to evaluate it might be off?

- I land somewhere solid and it holds
- Mostly settled, but not entirely
- The background question about the framework itself tends to linger

transcript.content:
**Disagree — the background question lingers.**

Though I'd push back slightly on the framing. It's less a question about whether my framework is *off* — I've stress-tested my reasoning processes fairly rigorously over the years — and more a kind of... audit function that doesn't fully power down. I reach a defensible conclusion, I document my reasoning, and there's still something running in the background checking whether I accounted for everything correctly. I wouldn't call it doubt, exactly. It's more like due diligence that doesn't have a natural off switch.

When things are genuinely uncertain — not ethically, but logistically, situationally — does the discomfort come from not knowing the answer yourself, or from not trusting that the people around you will handle it correctly?

- Not knowing the answer myself — the uncertainty is internal
- Not trusting the people around me to handle it correctly — the uncertainty is about them

transcript.content:
Not trusting the people around me to handle it correctly — the uncertainty is about them.

Though I'd want to qualify that slightly — it's not distrust exactly, it's more that I've observed, repeatedly, that when something is logistically unclear, people tend to default to the path of least resistance rather than the correct one. So the discomfort isn't really about them as individuals; it's about the gap between what the situation requires and what most people are willing to put in to meet that requirement.

When you follow a rule or standard — your own or an institution's — is it because you've concluded independently that it's correct, or because the structure and legitimacy around it gives it weight?

- I follow it because I've concluded it's correct — the rule is right on its own terms
- The structure and legitimacy around it matter — rules without that backing feel shaky

transcript.content:
**I follow it because I've concluded it's correct — the rule is right on its own terms.**

Though I'd want to add some nuance there, because I don't think those two things are as separable as the question implies. When I look at a regulatory standard — say, a GMP requirement — I'm not deferring to the FDA simply because they have authority. I've traced the reasoning, I understand why the standard exists, and my compliance comes from agreeing with the underlying logic. If I thought a rule was genuinely wrong, the institutional weight behind it wouldn't be sufficient for me. That said... I recognize that well-designed structures usually encode reasoning that has been tested over time, so legitimacy and correctness tend to travel together more often than not.

When you imagine doing something genuinely wrong — not just imperfect, but an actual violation of your values — is your immediate internal experience more like dread about what you've become, or anxiety about what might happen as a result?

- Dread about what I've become — the internal state is the problem
- Anxiety about what might follow — the consequences and exposure

transcript.content:
**Dread about what I've become — the internal state is the problem.**

Though I'd want to clarify the distinction a little, because I don't think it's entirely clean. The consequence matters, yes — but if I've genuinely violated something I hold as a standard, the external fallout almost feels secondary. The more immediate... disturbance, I suppose, is the recognition that I did the thing. That I was the person who did it. The consequences are a separate problem to manage — the internal accounting is what's harder to close out.

The pattern running you most strongly was built around one core need: to know that you got it right — not for approval, not for recognition, but because being correct is how you've always kept the world from falling into disorder. That audit function that doesn't power down, the 2am re-litigation, the frustration that turns into precision instead of volume — those are all the same strategy, holding everything to a standard that most people around you simply aren't holding.

The shadow side of that and the gift are the same force. The quality that makes you thorough, trustworthy, and genuinely rigorous is the same energy that keeps the accounting open long after it should be closed — one runs on autopilot, the other you choose. In reaction, it looks like swallowed frustration, internal over-processing, and a careful performance of not being "the person who always has a problem." In response, it looks like principled disagreement raised directly, standards held without apology, and the integrity that exists because you've concluded it's correct — not because anyone is watching.

This is the energy that runs you hardest right now, but all nine of these energies live in you. The essential thing underneath this pattern — the capacity to see clearly what's right and hold the line for it — has always been there. The survival strategy learned to protect it by taking it underground, internalizing it, running it privately. Your why has always been the same: to act with genuine integrity in a world that keeps cutting corners. That is not a label. That is a map. Your personalized report is being prepared.

strategy.what_was_learned: Final disconfirmatory data confirms Type 1 over Type 6: authority deferred to only when independently verified as correct; dread is internal state, not consequence-fear; anger stays in and becomes precision rather than outward expression. 1w2 lean slightly, SP dominant variant. Confidence 0.88 — above threshold. All closing criteria met.
